jnNhwJ3Ih_s.title: Learn to Read and Pronounce Bemba Words
jnNhwJ3Ih_s.prompt_used: This is a structured Bemba language lesson from KitweOnline. The instructor teaches Bemba vocabulary, pronunciation, and phrases. Common Bemba words: mulishani (how are you), mwabombeni (how are you d...

segments.text: Most of the words are based around the vowels A, E, I, O, U. The Bemba sound for them is
A, E, I, O, U. And most words are formed by consonants where we mix another letter
of the alphabet with the vowel. Primary school children are sometimes taught a trick to remember
these combinations and it also helps children to learn the alphabet and you go from the
letter A to Z but taking out the vowels A, E, I, O, U and then mixing them with the other
letters of the alphabet. So we'll start with the letter B, ba, be, bi, bo, bu. Letter C, cha, che,
chi, cho, chu. Letter D, da, de, di, do, du. There's a song about that and it goes like this.
Da, de, di, do, du. Da, de, fa, fe, fi, fo, fu.
Da, de, di, do, du. Da, de, fa, fe, fi, fo, fu.
Letter E, ka, ke, ki, ko, ku. Ka, ke, ki, ko, ku. Ka, ke, ki, ko, ku. Ka, ke, L.
La, le, di, lo, lu. La, le, di, lo, lu. La, le, di, lo, lu. La, le, Di, li lo lu. La,
le, Di, li lo lu. La, le, Di, lo, lu. La, le, Di, lo, lu. La, le, Di, lo, lu. La, le,
Di, lo, lu. La, le, Di, lo, lu. La, le, Pi. Pa, pe, pi, po, pu. Pa, pe, pi, po, pu. Pa,
pe, pi, po, pu. Pa, pe. Sa, se, si, so, su. Sa, se, si, so, su. Sa, se, si, so, su. Sa,
se, ti. Ta, te, ti, to, tu. Ta, te, ti, to, tu. Ta, te, ti, to, tu. Ta, te. Wa, we, wi,
wi, wo, wu. Wa, we, wi, wo, wu. Wa, we, wi, wo, wu. Wa, we. Y. Ya, ye, yi, yo, yu. Ya,
ye, yi, yo, yu. Ya, ye, yi, yo, yu. Ya, ye. Z. Sa, se, si, so, su. Sa, se, si, so, su. Sa, se, si, so, su. Sa,
se. Now that you are comfortable with syllables that are made up of a consonant and a vowel,
take your time and look at some written Bemba words. Have fun working out how to pronounce them
using the method you've just learnt here. This brings us to the end of this lesson.

segments.text: Hello and welcome to Bemba Lesson 12, Introducing Yourself.
Hello.
Mwaboleni mkwai.
How are you?
I am fine, thanks.
My name is Jane.
What is your name?
I have come from Kitwe.
I have come from London.
I live in London.

RnGkyhNyPsk.title: 38-How to Ask for Directions in Bemba
RnGkyhNyPsk.prompt_used: This is a structured Bemba language lesson from KitweOnline. The instructor teaches Bemba vocabulary, pronunciation, and phrases. Common Bemba words: mulishani (how are you), mwabombeni (how are you d...

segments.text: Welcome to our lesson on asking for directions in Bemba. Today, we'll use fun illustrations to learn key phrases and vocabulary. Let's get started.
Before asking for directions, it's polite to greet the person. Here's how.
Njeleleniko and njehlehlehniko means excuse me.
Njipusheko and njipusheko means may I ask. Use these phrases to start your conversation politely.
Asking, where is, to ask where a place is, use the phrase ku, place, nikwisa. Ku place, nikwisa.
For example, ku maliketi nikwisa? Where is the market? Ku famasi nikwisa? Where is the pharmacy?
Ku station nikwisa? Where is the bus station? This is your go-to phrase for finding any place.
Here are some common places you might ask about. Maliketi, market famasi, pharmacy station,
bus station isukulu, school practice, saying these words so you can ask for directions confidently.
Now, let's learn some direction words. Kukulio, kukulio. Right, kukuso, kukuso.
Left, pantanshi, pantanshi. Straight ahead.
These words will help you understand the directions you are given.
When someone gives you directions, they might say, muye pantanshi, muye pantanshi. Go straight.
Koneka kukulio, koneka kukulio. Turn right. Koneka kukuso, koneka kukuso. Turn left.
Practice these phrases so you can follow directions easily.
To ask if a place is near or far, use these words. Mupepi, mupepi. Near patali, patali, far.
For example, nimupepi, nangupatali. Is it near or far? This will help you know how much farther
you need to go. Here are two important words for movement. Iminina, iminina. Stop
here. Enda, enda. Go. For example, iminina pano. Stop here. Enda pantanshi. Go straight.
These words are useful when following directions. Let's practice what we've learned.
Match the Bemba phrases with their English meanings. Mwendelele pantanshi. Go straight.
Kumaliketini kwisa. Where is the market? Iminina pano. Stop here. Koneka kukulio.
Turn right. Ndolekwi. Which way do I go? Great job. Keep practicing to master these phrases.
Congratulations. You've learned how to ask for and understand directions in Bemba.
Here's your homework. Practice asking for directions to a place in Bemba.
Write down the response you get. Translate five new Bemba phrases into English and vice versa.
Keep practicing and soon you'll be navigating like a pro. See you next time.
